content: Consider two decision problems $${Q_1},{Q_2}$$ such that $${Q_1}$$ reduces in polynomial time to $$3$$-$$SAT$$ and $$3$$-$$SAT$$ reduces in polynomial time to $${Q_2}.$$ Then which one of the following is consistent with the above statement?

options.content: Both $${Q_1}$$ and $${Q_2}$$ are in $$NP.$$
Both $${Q_1}$$ and $${Q_2}$$ are $$NP$$ hard.

content:
Which of the following statements are TRUE?

1. The problem of determining whether there exists a cycle in an undirected graph is in P.

2. The problem of determining whether there exists a cycle in an undirected graph is in NP.

3. If a problem A is NP−Complete, there exists a non-deterministic polynomial time algorithm to solve A.

options.content: 1 , 2 and 3
1 and 2 only
2 and 3 only
1 and 3 only

content: Let $${\pi _A}$$ be a problem that belongs to the class NP. Then which one of the following is TRUE?

options.content: There is no polynomial time algorithm for $${\pi _A}$$
If $${\pi _A}$$  can be solved deterministically in polynomial time, then P = NP
If $${\pi _A}$$  is NP-hard, then it is NP-complete.
$${\pi _A}$$  may be undecidable.

content: Let S be an NP-complete problem and Q and R be two other problems not known to be in NP. Q is polynomial time reducible to S and S is polynomial-time reducible to R. Which one of the following statements is true?

options.content: R is NP-complete
R is NP-hard
Q is NP-complete
Q is NP-hard
both in P
both NP-complete
NP-complete and in P respectively
undecidable and NP-complete respectively

content: Ram and Shyam have been asked to show that a certain problem Π is NP-complete. Ram shows a polynomial time reduction from the 3-SAT problem to Π, and Shyam shows a polynomial time reduction from Π to 3-SAT. Which of the following can be inferred from these reductions ?

options.content: Π is NP-hard but not NP-complete
Π is in NP, but is not NP-complete
Π is NP-complete
Π is neither NP-hard, nor in NP

content:
Suppose a polynomial time algorithm is discovered that correctly computes the largest clique in a
given graph. In this scenario, which one of the following represents the correct Venn diagram of the
complexity classes P, NP and NP Complete (NPC)?

Consider the decision problem 2CNFSAT defined as follows:

{ $$\Phi $$ | $$\Phi $$ is a satisfiable propositional formula in CNF with at most two literal per clause}

For example, $$\Phi = \left( {{x_1} \vee {x_2}} \right) \wedge \left( {{x_1} \vee {{\overline x }_3}} \right) \wedge \left( {{x_2} \vee {x_4}} \right)$$ is a Boolean formula and it is in 2CNFSAT.

The decision problem 2CNFSAT is

options.content: NP-Complete.
Solvable in polynomial time by reduction to directed graph reachability.
Solvable in constant time since any input instance is satisfiable.
NP-Hard, but not NP-complete.

content:
The subset-sum problem is defined as follows:
Given a set S of n positive integers and a positive integer W, determine whether there is a subset of S Whose elements sum to W. An algorithm Q solves this problem in O(nW) time. Which of the following statements is false?

options.content: Q solves the subset-sum problem in polynomial time when the input is encoded in unary
Q solves the subset-sum problem in polynomial time when the input is encoded in binary
The subset sum problem belongs to the class NP
The subset sum problem is NP-hard

content: Let SHAM<sub>3</sub> be the problem of finding a Hamiltonian cycle in a graph G = (V, E)  with |V|  divisible by 3 and DHAM<sub>3</sub>  be the problem of determining if a Hamiltonian cycle exists in such graphs. Which one of the following is true?

options.content: Both DHAM<sub>3</sub> and SHAM<sub>3</sub> are NP-hard
SHAM<sub>3</sub> is NP-hard, but DHAM<sub>3</sub> is not
DHAM<sub>3</sub> is NP-hard, but SHAM<sub>3</sub> is not
Neither DHAM<sub>3</sub> nor SHAM<sub>3</sub> is NP-hard

content: Consider three decision problems P1, P2 and P3. It is known that P1 is decidable and P2 is undecidable. Which one of the following is TRUE?

options.content: P<sub>3</sub> is decidable if P<sub>1</sub> is reducible to P<sub>3</sub>
P<sub>3</sub> is undecidable if P<sub>3</sub> is reducible to P<sub>2</sub>
P<sub>3</sub> is undecidable if P<sub>2</sub> is reducible to P<sub>3</sub>
P<sub>3</sub> is decidable if P<sub>3</sub> is reducible to P<sub>2</sub> 's complement

content: Which of the following problems is not NP-hard?

options.content: Hamiltonian circuit problem
The 0/1 Knapsack problem
Finding bi-connected components of a graph
The graph coloring problem
